instruction: How should I use std::optional in C++ for expressing nullable values? Show patterns for chaining operations on optional values.
output:
<think>
std::optional<T> (C++17) represents a value that may or may not be present. It's the C++ equivalent of Rust's Option<T> or Haskell's Maybe. Before optional, the alternatives were: returning a bool + out parameter, using sentinel values (like -1 or nullptr), or throwing exceptions for "not found" cases.

Key patterns:
1. Return optional from functions instead of sentinel values
2. Monadic operations (C++23): and_then, transform, or_else
3. value_or() for defaults
4. Chaining with pattern matching
5. Using optional in data structures for nullable fields

Before C++23, chaining optionals required verbose if-checks. C++23 added monadic operations that make this much cleaner.

Important performance note: optional<T> stores T inline (no heap allocation). Its size is sizeof(T) + 1 byte (for the has-value flag), with alignment padding. For small types, this is fine. For large types, consider optional<unique_ptr<T>> if you want to avoid the inline storage.

optional<reference> doesn't exist in the standard (controversial). Use pointers or std::reference_wrapper for nullable references.
</think>

Here's how to use std::optional effectively in modern C++:

```cpp
#include <optional>
#include <string>
#include <iostream>
#include <vector>
#include <map>
#include <functional>

using namespace std;

// ============================================================
// 1. Basic: optional return values instead of sentinels
// ============================================================

// BAD: sentinel value (-1 for "not found")
int find_index_bad(const vector<int>& v, int target) {
    for (size_t i = 0; i < v.size(); ++i) {
        if (v[i] == target) return static_cast<int>(i);
    }
    return -1; // Magic value — what if -1 is a valid index? (it isn't, but the type allows it)
}

// GOOD: optional makes the absence explicit in the type
optional<size_t> find_index(const vector<int>& v, int target) {
    for (size_t i = 0; i < v.size(); ++i) {
        if (v[i] == target) return i;
    }
    return nullopt; // Explicitly "not found"
}

void basic_usage() {
    vector<int> data = {10, 20, 30, 40, 50};
    
    // Check with has_value()
    auto idx = find_index(data, 30);
    if (idx.has_value()) {
        cout << "Found at index: " << idx.value() << endl;
    }
    
    // Shorter: implicit bool conversion
    if (auto idx2 = find_index(data, 99)) {
        cout << "Found: " << *idx2 << endl;
    } else {
        cout << "Not found" << endl;
    }
    
    // Default value with value_or()
    size_t pos = find_index(data, 99).value_or(0);
    cout << "Position (default 0): " << pos << endl;
}

// ============================================================
// 2. Optional in data structures
// ============================================================

struct UserProfile {
    string name;
    string email;
    optional<string> phone;        // Not everyone has a phone
    optional<string> bio;          // Optional bio
    optional<int> age;             // Optional age
};

void print_profile(const UserProfile& user) {
    cout << "Name: " << user.name << endl;
    cout << "Email: " << user.email << endl;
    cout << "Phone: " << user.phone.value_or("not provided") << endl;
    if (user.bio) {
        cout << "Bio: " << *user.bio << endl;
    }
    if (user.age) {
        cout << "Age: " << *user.age << endl;
    }
}

// ============================================================
// 3. Chaining optionals (pre-C++23)
// ============================================================

struct Config {
    map<string, string> values;
    
    optional<string> get(const string& key) const {
        auto it = values.find(key);
        if (it != values.end()) return it->second;
        return nullopt;
    }
};

optional<int> parse_int(const string& s) {
    try {
        return stoi(s);
    } catch (...) {
        return nullopt;
    }
}

// Pre-C++23: manual chaining is verbose
optional<int> get_port_verbose(const Config& config) {
    auto value = config.get("port");
    if (!value) return nullopt;
    return parse_int(*value);
}

// Helper: and_then for pre-C++23
template<typename T, typename F>
auto and_then(const optional<T>& opt, F&& f) -> decltype(f(*opt)) {
    if (opt) return f(*opt);
    return nullopt;
}

template<typename T, typename F>
auto transform(const optional<T>& opt, F&& f) -> optional<decltype(f(*opt))> {
    if (opt) return f(*opt);
    return nullopt;
}

// Cleaner chaining with helpers
optional<int> get_port_clean(const Config& config) {
    return and_then(config.get("port"), parse_int);
}

// ============================================================
// 4. C++23 monadic operations (if available)
// ============================================================

#if __cplusplus >= 202302L
optional<int> get_port_cpp23(const Config& config) {
    return config.get("port")
        .and_then(parse_int)           // optional<string> → optional<int>
        .transform([](int p) {         // optional<int> → optional<int>
            return p > 0 ? p : 8080;
        })
        .or_else([]() -> optional<int> { // if empty, provide default
            return 8080;
        });
}
#endif

// ============================================================
// 5. Pattern: optional with structured bindings
// ============================================================

pair<optional<string>, optional<string>> split_name(const string& full_name) {
    auto space = full_name.find(' ');
    if (space == string::npos) {
        return {full_name, nullopt};
    }
    return {full_name.substr(0, space), full_name.substr(space + 1)};
}

void structured_binding_demo() {
    auto [first, last] = split_name("Jane Doe");
    cout << "First: " << *first << endl;
    cout << "Last: " << last.value_or("(none)") << endl;
}

int main() {
    basic_usage();
    
    UserProfile user{"Alice", "alice@example.com", "+1234567890", nullopt, 30};
    print_profile(user);
    
    Config config{{"port", "8080"}, {"host", "localhost"}};
    auto port = get_port_clean(config);
    cout << "\nPort: " << port.value_or(80) << endl;
    
    structured_binding_demo();
    return 0;
}
```

Use std::optional for values that may legitimately be absent — function return values, nullable fields, configuration lookups. Prefer value_or() for defaults, and_then() for chaining (C++23 or polyfill), and implicit bool conversion for checks. Never use optional<bool> (confusing) or optional<reference> (not allowed — use pointers).

instruction: How do I use std::variant in C++ for type-safe tagged unions? Show visitor patterns and error handling.
output:
<think>
std::variant<Types...> (C++17) is a type-safe tagged union. It holds exactly one value of one of the listed types at any time. Unlike a C union, variant tracks which type is active and prevents accessing the wrong type.

Key patterns:
1. std::visit with overloaded lambda visitors
2. std::get_if for safe type checking
3. Error handling with variant<Value, Error>
4. State machines with variant
5. The overloaded{} pattern for combining lambdas

The overloaded{} pattern is so common it deserves emphasis — it combines multiple lambdas into a single visitor using aggregate initialization + CTAD.

Performance: variant stores data inline (like a union) with a type index. No heap allocation. sizeof(variant<A,B,C>) = max(sizeof(A), sizeof(B), sizeof(C)) + type index (usually 1-4 bytes).

Key gotcha: variant can be in a "valueless by exception" state if a constructor throws during assignment. Check with variant.valueless_by_exception().

Common patterns: variant as a result type (like Rust's Result<T,E>), variant for polymorphism without inheritance, variant for state machines.
</think>

Here's how to use std::variant for type-safe tagged unions:

```cpp
#include <variant>
#include <string>
#include <iostream>
#include <vector>
#include <cassert>

using namespace std;

// ============================================================
// The overloaded{} pattern — essential for variant visitors
// ============================================================

template<class... Ts>
struct overloaded : Ts... { using Ts::operator()...; };
template<class... Ts>
overloaded(Ts...) -> overloaded<Ts...>;  // CTAD guide

// ============================================================
// 1. Basic variant usage
// ============================================================

using JsonValue = variant<nullptr_t, bool, int, double, string, vector<int>>;

void print_json(const JsonValue& value) {
    visit(overloaded{
        [](nullptr_t) { cout << "null"; },
        [](bool b)     { cout << (b ? "true" : "false"); },
        [](int i)      { cout << i; },
        [](double d)   { cout << d; },
        [](const string& s) { cout << '"' << s << '"'; },
        [](const vector<int>& v) {
            cout << "[";
            for (size_t i = 0; i < v.size(); ++i) {
                if (i > 0) cout << ", ";
                cout << v[i];
            }
            cout << "]";
        },
    }, value);
}

void basic_demo() {
    JsonValue v1 = 42;
    JsonValue v2 = "hello"s;
    JsonValue v3 = vector<int>{1, 2, 3};
    JsonValue v4 = nullptr;
    
    for (const auto& v : {v1, v2, v4}) {
        print_json(v);
        cout << endl;
    }
    print_json(v3);
    cout << endl;
}

// ============================================================
// 2. Result type — variant<Value, Error>
// ============================================================

struct ParseError {
    string message;
    int position;
};

struct NetworkError {
    int code;
    string reason;
};

using Error = variant<ParseError, NetworkError>;

template<typename T>
using Result = variant<T, Error>;

Result<int> parse_number(const string& input) {
    try {
        size_t pos;
        int value = stoi(input, &pos);
        if (pos != input.size()) {
            return Error{ParseError{"Trailing characters", static_cast<int>(pos)}};
        }
        return value;
    } catch (const exception&) {
        return Error{ParseError{"Invalid number", 0}};
    }
}

void handle_result(const Result<int>& result) {
    visit(overloaded{
        [](int value) {
            cout << "Success: " << value << endl;
        },
        [](const Error& err) {
            visit(overloaded{
                [](const ParseError& e) {
                    cout << "Parse error at " << e.position << ": " << e.message << endl;
                },
                [](const NetworkError& e) {
                    cout << "Network error " << e.code << ": " << e.reason << endl;
                },
            }, err);
        },
    }, result);
}

// ============================================================
// 3. State machine with variant
// ============================================================

struct Idle {};
struct Connecting { string host; int port; };
struct Connected { int socket_fd; };
struct Disconnecting { string reason; };

using ConnectionState = variant<Idle, Connecting, Connected, Disconnecting>;

class Connection {
    ConnectionState state_ = Idle{};
    
public:
    void connect(const string& host, int port) {
        visit(overloaded{
            [&](Idle) {
                cout << "Connecting to " << host << ":" << port << endl;
                state_ = Connecting{host, port};
            },
            [](const auto&) {
                cout << "Cannot connect: not in Idle state" << endl;
            },
        }, state_);
    }
    
    void on_connected(int fd) {
        visit(overloaded{
            [&](Connecting&) {
                cout << "Connected! fd=" << fd << endl;
                state_ = Connected{fd};
            },
            [](const auto&) {
                cout << "Unexpected connected event" << endl;
            },
        }, state_);
    }
    
    void disconnect(const string& reason) {
        visit(overloaded{
            [&](Connected&) {
                cout << "Disconnecting: " << reason << endl;
                state_ = Disconnecting{reason};
            },
            [](const auto&) {
                cout << "Not connected" << endl;
            },
        }, state_);
    }
    
    string status() const {
        return visit(overloaded{
            [](const Idle&) -> string { return "idle"; },
            [](const Connecting& c) -> string { return "connecting to " + c.host; },
            [](const Connected& c) -> string { return "connected (fd=" + to_string(c.socket_fd) + ")"; },
            [](const Disconnecting& d) -> string { return "disconnecting: " + d.reason; },
        }, state_);
    }
};

// ============================================================
// 4. Safe access patterns
// ============================================================

void safe_access_demo() {
    variant<int, string, double> v = "hello"s;
    
    // get_if: returns pointer or nullptr (safe)
    if (auto* s = get_if<string>(&v)) {
        cout << "It's a string: " << *s << endl;
    }
    
    // get: throws bad_variant_access if wrong type
    try {
        int i = get<int>(v); // Wrong type!
    } catch (const bad_variant_access& e) {
        cout << "Bad access: " << e.what() << endl;
    }
    
    // index(): which type is active (0-based)
    cout << "Active index: " << v.index() << endl; // 1 (string)
    
    // holds_alternative: type check
    if (holds_alternative<string>(v)) {
        cout << "Holds a string" << endl;
    }
}

int main() {
    cout << "=== JSON values ==="  << endl;
    basic_demo();
    
    cout << "\n=== Result type ===" << endl;
    handle_result(parse_number("42"));
    handle_result(parse_number("abc"));
    handle_result(parse_number("42xyz"));
    
    cout << "\n=== State machine ===" << endl;
    Connection conn;
    cout << "Status: " << conn.status() << endl;
    conn.connect("localhost", 8080);
    cout << "Status: " << conn.status() << endl;
    conn.on_connected(5);
    cout << "Status: " << conn.status() << endl;
    conn.disconnect("user requested");
    cout << "Status: " << conn.status() << endl;
    
    cout << "\n=== Safe access ===" << endl;
    safe_access_demo();
    
    return 0;
}
```

std::variant replaces C unions with type safety. Use the overloaded{} pattern with std::visit for clean handling, variant<T, Error> as a result type, and variant for encoding state machines. Prefer get_if over get to avoid exceptions, and use holds_alternative for type checks.

instruction: Explain std::expected (C++23) and how it compares to std::optional and exceptions for error handling.
output:
<think>
std::expected<T, E> (C++23) is C++'s answer to Rust's Result<T, E>. It holds either a value of type T (success) or an error of type E (failure). This fills a gap between optional (only signals presence/absence, no error info) and exceptions (which have overhead and control flow issues).

Comparison:
- optional<T>: value or nothing. No error context. Good for "not found" cases.
- expected<T, E>: value or typed error. Carries error information. Good for operations that can fail in multiple ways.
- exceptions: value or throw. Implicit propagation. Good for truly exceptional cases.

Performance:
- expected: zero overhead for success path (just like returning T directly). Error path stores E inline.
- exceptions: zero overhead for success path (table-based EH), but very expensive for error path (stack unwinding, ~1000x slower).
- So expected and exceptions have similar success-path performance, but expected is much cheaper on the error path.

Monadic operations (C++23): expected has and_then, transform, or_else, transform_error — same as optional's monadic operations. This enables chaining without verbose if-checks.

I should show: basic usage, monadic chaining, comparison with exceptions and optional, and practical error handling patterns.
</think>

Here's how std::expected works and when to use it:

```cpp
// Requires C++23: g++ -std=c++23
#include <expected>
#include <string>
#include <iostream>
#include <fstream>
#include <system_error>
#include <charconv>

using namespace std;

// ============================================================
// 1. Basic std::expected usage
// ============================================================

enum class ParseError {
    EmptyInput,
    InvalidFormat,
    OutOfRange,
};

string to_string(ParseError e) {
    switch (e) {
        case ParseError::EmptyInput:    return "empty input";
        case ParseError::InvalidFormat: return "invalid format";
        case ParseError::OutOfRange:    return "out of range";
    }
    return "unknown";
}

expected<int, ParseError> parse_int(string_view input) {
    if (input.empty()) {
        return unexpected(ParseError::EmptyInput);
    }
    
    int result;
    auto [ptr, ec] = from_chars(input.data(), input.data() + input.size(), result);
    
    if (ec == errc::invalid_argument) {
        return unexpected(ParseError::InvalidFormat);
    }
    if (ec == errc::result_out_of_range) {
        return unexpected(ParseError::OutOfRange);
    }
    if (ptr != input.data() + input.size()) {
        return unexpected(ParseError::InvalidFormat);
    }
    
    return result; // Success
}

void basic_usage() {
    auto result = parse_int("42");
    
    // Check and access
    if (result.has_value()) {
        cout << "Value: " << result.value() << endl;
    }
    
    // Or with * operator
    if (result) {
        cout << "Value: " << *result << endl;
    }
    
    // Error case
    auto err_result = parse_int("abc");
    if (!err_result) {
        cout << "Error: " << to_string(err_result.error()) << endl;
    }
    
    // Default value
    int val = parse_int("abc").value_or(-1);
    cout << "With default: " << val << endl;
}

// ============================================================
// 2. Monadic operations (chaining)
// ============================================================

struct Config {
    int port;
    string host;
    int max_connections;
};

expected<string, string> read_file(const string& path) {
    ifstream f(path);
    if (!f.is_open()) {
        return unexpected("Cannot open file: " + path);
    }
    return string(istreambuf_iterator<char>(f), {});
}

expected<int, string> parse_port(const string& content) {
    // Simplified: just parse the first line as a number
    auto result = parse_int(content.substr(0, content.find('\n')));
    if (!result) {
        return unexpected("Invalid port: " + to_string(result.error()));
    }
    if (*result < 1 || *result > 65535) {
        return unexpected("Port out of range: " + std::to_string(*result));
    }
    return *result;
}

// C++23 monadic chaining:
expected<int, string> load_port_from_file(const string& path) {
    return read_file(path)
        .and_then(parse_port)  // If success, chain to next operation
        .transform([](int port) { // Transform the success value
            return port > 0 ? port : 8080;
        })
        .or_else([](string err) -> expected<int, string> {
            cerr << "Warning: " << err << ", using default" << endl;
            return 8080;  // Recover with default
        });
}

// ============================================================
// 3. Comparison: expected vs optional vs exceptions
// ============================================================

// With OPTIONAL: no error information
optional<int> find_user_id_optional(const string& name) {
    if (name == "alice") return 1;
    return nullopt; // Why did it fail? Not found? Invalid? DB error?
}

// With EXPECTED: typed error information
enum class LookupError { NotFound, DatabaseError, InvalidInput };

expected<int, LookupError> find_user_id_expected(const string& name) {
    if (name.empty()) return unexpected(LookupError::InvalidInput);
    if (name == "alice") return 1;
    return unexpected(LookupError::NotFound);
}

// With EXCEPTIONS: implicit propagation
int find_user_id_exception(const string& name) {
    if (name.empty()) throw invalid_argument("empty name");
    if (name == "alice") return 1;
    throw runtime_error("user not found: " + name);
}

/*
Decision guide:

  optional<T>      → "might not have a value" (no error context needed)
                     find(), lookup(), cache miss

  expected<T, E>   → "might fail, and here's why" (error context needed)
                     parse(), validate(), file I/O, network
                     Hot paths where exceptions are too expensive

  exceptions       → "this shouldn't normally happen" (truly exceptional)
                     Out of memory, programming errors, hardware failures
                     When you want automatic propagation up the call stack

Performance:
  optional:   zero overhead (inline storage)
  expected:   zero overhead on success path, cheap error path
  exceptions: zero overhead on success path, VERY expensive error path
              (~1000x slower than expected for error case)
*/

// ============================================================
// 4. Rich error types with expected
// ============================================================

struct DetailedError {
    int code;
    string message;
    string source;
    
    static DetailedError not_found(string what) {
        return {404, what + " not found", "lookup"};
    }
    static DetailedError invalid(string what) {
        return {400, "Invalid: " + what, "validation"};
    }
    static DetailedError internal(string what) {
        return {500, what, "internal"};
    }
};

expected<string, DetailedError> get_user_email(int user_id) {
    if (user_id <= 0) {
        return unexpected(DetailedError::invalid("user_id must be positive"));
    }
    if (user_id == 1) {
        return "alice@example.com";
    }
    return unexpected(DetailedError::not_found("user " + std::to_string(user_id)));
}

void rich_error_demo() {
    auto result = get_user_email(42);
    if (!result) {
        const auto& err = result.error();
        cout << "Error " << err.code << " [" << err.source << "]: " << err.message << endl;
    }
}

int main() {
    cout << "=== Basic ==="  << endl;
    basic_usage();
    
    cout << "\n=== Rich errors ===" << endl;
    rich_error_demo();
    
    cout << "\n=== File loading (monadic) ===" << endl;
    auto port = load_port_from_file("config.txt");
    cout << "Port: " << *port << endl;
    
    return 0;
}
```

std::expected<T, E> fills the gap between optional (no error info) and exceptions (expensive error path). Use it for operations that regularly fail and need error context. The C++23 monadic operations (and_then, transform, or_else) enable clean chaining without verbose if-checks, similar to Rust's Result combinators.

instruction: Show me how to use structured bindings and if constexpr patterns in modern C++.
output:
<think>
Structured bindings (C++17) decompose aggregates, tuples, and pairs into named variables. if constexpr (C++17) enables compile-time branching that eliminates dead code entirely. Together, these make C++ code more readable and generic.

Structured bindings work with:
1. Arrays: auto [a, b, c] = array;
2. Tuple-like: auto [x, y] = pair/tuple;
3. Structs: auto [field1, field2] = struct; (all public members)
4. Maps: for (auto& [key, value] : map)

if constexpr enables:
1. Type-based specialization in templates without SFINAE
2. Eliminating code paths that would fail to compile for certain types
3. Compile-time type inspection with type_traits

These are relatively straightforward features but the combinations are powerful. I should show practical patterns that combine both.
</think>

Here's how to use structured bindings and if constexpr in modern C++:

```cpp
#include <iostream>
#include <map>
#include <string>
#include <tuple>
#include <type_traits>
#include <vector>
#include <optional>
#include <utility>

using namespace std;

// ============================================================
// 1. Structured bindings basics
// ============================================================

struct Point { double x, y, z; };

void structured_bindings_demo() {
    // From structs (all public members)
    Point p{1.0, 2.0, 3.0};
    auto [x, y, z] = p;
    cout << "Point: " << x << ", " << y << ", " << z << endl;
    
    // From pairs
    pair<string, int> kv{"age", 30};
    auto [key, value] = kv;
    cout << key << " = " << value << endl;
    
    // From tuples
    auto [name, score, passed] = make_tuple("Alice"s, 95.5, true);
    cout << name << ": " << score << " (" << (passed ? "pass" : "fail") << ")" << endl;
    
    // From arrays
    int arr[] = {10, 20, 30};
    auto [a, b, c] = arr;
    cout << "Array: " << a << ", " << b << ", " << c << endl;
    
    // Mutable bindings with reference
    auto& [px, py, pz] = p; // References to p's fields
    px = 100.0; // Modifies p.x
    cout << "Modified p.x: " << p.x << endl;
}

// ============================================================
// 2. Map iteration with structured bindings
// ============================================================

void map_iteration() {
    map<string, vector<int>> scores = {
        {"Alice", {95, 87, 92}},
        {"Bob",   {78, 82, 90}},
        {"Carol", {88, 91, 85}},
    };
    
    // Before C++17:
    // for (const auto& pair : scores) {
    //     cout << pair.first << ": " << pair.second.size() << endl;
    // }
    
    // C++17: much cleaner
    for (const auto& [name, grades] : scores) {
        double avg = 0;
        for (int g : grades) avg += g;
        avg /= grades.size();
        cout << name << ": avg = " << avg << endl;
    }
    
    // With insert result
    auto [it, inserted] = scores.insert({"Dave", {75, 80}});
    cout << "Dave " << (inserted ? "inserted" : "already exists") << endl;
    
    // With find
    if (auto it = scores.find("Alice"); it != scores.end()) {
        auto& [name, grades] = *it;
        cout << name << " has " << grades.size() << " grades" << endl;
    }
}

// ============================================================
// 3. if constexpr — compile-time branching
// ============================================================

// Generic print that handles different types
template<typename T>
void smart_print(const T& value) {
    if constexpr (is_integral_v<T>) {
        cout << "Integer: " << value << " (hex: 0x" << hex << value << dec << ")" << endl;
    } else if constexpr (is_floating_point_v<T>) {
        cout << "Float: " << fixed << value << endl;
    } else if constexpr (is_same_v<T, string>) {
        cout << "String (" << value.length() << " chars): " << value << endl;
    } else if constexpr (is_pointer_v<T>) {
        cout << "Pointer: " << (value ? "non-null" : "null") << endl;
    } else {
        cout << "Unknown type" << endl;
    }
    // Each branch that doesn't match is COMPLETELY removed from compilation.
    // No "if" at runtime — the compiler selects the right branch.
}

// ============================================================
// 4. if constexpr for SFINAE-free specialization
// ============================================================

// Check if type has a .size() method
template<typename T, typename = void>
struct has_size : false_type {};

template<typename T>
struct has_size<T, void_t<decltype(declval<T>().size())>> : true_type {};

template<typename Container>
void describe_container(const Container& c) {
    if constexpr (has_size<Container>::value) {
        cout << "Container with " << c.size() << " elements";
    } else {
        cout << "Object (no .size() method)";
    }
    
    // Check if iterable
    if constexpr (requires { begin(c); end(c); }) {  // C++20 requires
        cout << ", iterable";
    }
    
    cout << endl;
}

// ============================================================
// 5. Combining structured bindings + if constexpr
// ============================================================

template<typename T>
auto try_parse(const string& input) -> pair<optional<T>, string> {
    if constexpr (is_same_v<T, int>) {
        try {
            return {stoi(input), ""};
        } catch (const exception& e) {
            return {nullopt, e.what()};
        }
    } else if constexpr (is_same_v<T, double>) {
        try {
            return {stod(input), ""};
        } catch (const exception& e) {
            return {nullopt, e.what()};
        }
    } else if constexpr (is_same_v<T, bool>) {
        if (input == "true" || input == "1") return {true, ""};
        if (input == "false" || input == "0") return {false, ""};
        return {nullopt, "not a boolean"};
    } else {
        static_assert(sizeof(T) == 0, "Unsupported type");
    }
}

void combined_demo() {
    // Structured binding + if constexpr result
    auto [value, error] = try_parse<int>("42");
    if (value) {
        cout << "Parsed int: " << *value << endl;
    } else {
        cout << "Error: " << error << endl;
    }
    
    auto [dval, derr] = try_parse<double>("3.14");
    if (dval) cout << "Parsed double: " << *dval << endl;
    
    auto [bval, berr] = try_parse<bool>("true");
    if (bval) cout << "Parsed bool: " << (*bval ? "true" : "false") << endl;
}

// ============================================================
// 6. Init-statement in if/switch (C++17)
// ============================================================

void init_statement_demo() {
    map<string, int> scores = {{"alice", 95}, {"bob", 87}};
    
    // Init + condition in one line
    if (auto it = scores.find("alice"); it != scores.end()) {
        auto [name, score] = *it;
        cout << name << " scored " << score << endl;
    }
    // it is not visible here — scoped to the if block
    
    // With structured binding in the init
    if (auto [it, ok] = scores.insert({"carol", 90}); ok) {
        cout << "Inserted " << it->first << endl;
    } else {
        cout << it->first << " already exists" << endl;
    }
}

int main() {
    cout << "=== Structured bindings ===" << endl;
    structured_bindings_demo();
    
    cout << "\n=== Map iteration ===" << endl;
    map_iteration();
    
    cout << "\n=== if constexpr ===" << endl;
    smart_print(42);
    smart_print(3.14);
    smart_print("hello"s);
    smart_print((int*)nullptr);
    
    cout << "\n=== Container description ===" << endl;
    describe_container(vector<int>{1, 2, 3});
    describe_container("hello"s);
    
    cout << "\n=== Combined ===" << endl;
    combined_demo();
    
    cout << "\n=== Init statements ===" << endl;
    init_statement_demo();
    
    return 0;
}
```

Structured bindings eliminate verbose `pair.first`/`pair.second` and make map iteration, tuple decomposition, and insert-result handling clean. if constexpr eliminates dead code branches at compile time, replacing SFINAE for type-based specialization. Together, they make template code readable and efficient.
